title: Planogram Execution
description: FMCG merchandising rules: how to place product so it sells, not just sits there.

steps.title: What a planogram is and why it matters
steps.content: A planogram is the SKU layout on a shelf based on shopper logic and brand share. Its job: drive sales through correct placement. Not 'pretty' — 'it sells'. Planograms are agreed with the retailer, approved by KAM, passed to the rep. Non-compliance = fine or loss of shelf space.
steps.key_takeaway: Planogram = document, not a suggestion. Breach = lost shelf.

steps.title: The golden shelf
steps.content: Shelf levels by value: 1) eye level (150-170 cm / 60-67 in) — gold, sells the most; 2) hand level (100-150 cm) — silver; 3) waist level (70-100 cm) — bronze; 4) floor level (< 70 cm) — 'the grave'. Bestsellers and premium — eye level. Kids' products — at kids' eye level (60-90 cm).
steps.key_takeaway: Eye level = gold. Floor level = the grave. Kids' products — lower, at kids' eye level.

steps.title: Facings rule
steps.content: Facing = units facing the shopper. Rule: 1 SKU = minimum 2 facings (otherwise invisible, no buyers). Bestseller — 4-6 facings. Brand's share of facings should match its share of category sales (80/20 rule). Too few facings on a bestseller = lost sales.
steps.key_takeaway: Minimum 2 facings. Below that, the product is invisible. Bestseller = 4-6 facings.

steps.title: FIFO rotation
steps.content: First In, First Out — first in goes out first. Put new stock at the BACK, pull old stock to the FRONT. Check dates: expiring in a week → promo zone or checkout zone. FIFO violation = write-offs (loss to distributor or store depending on contract). Do this every visit.
steps.key_takeaway: New to the back, old to the front. Always. Every visit. No exceptions.

steps.title: Grouping by shopper logic
steps.content: Shoppers think in categories, not brands. Group: by flavor (chocolate / strawberry / vanilla), by size (0.5L / 1L / 1.5L), by use case (breakfast / kids / diet). Within a group — by brand and price (low-to-high or the reverse, per retailer strategy).
steps.key_takeaway: Group first, then brands within the group. Shoppers search 'coffee', not 'Nestle'.

steps.title: Cross-merchandising
steps.content: Combining categories bought together: tea + cookies, pasta + sauce, beer + snacks, diapers + baby wipes. Placed 'one step apart' = +8-15% average basket. Negotiate with the store — a secondary pallet in the right zone. 'Better together' POSM amplifies the effect.
steps.key_takeaway: Tea + cookies = +8-15% basket. Cross-merch is the cheapest sales lift tool.

steps.title: Verification and logging
steps.content: After merchandising: photograph the shelf from three angles (wide, close-up, price tag). Compare with the planogram in the app — green check or red flag. Log deviations with reason (out of stock, no space, competitor on promo). CRM report on the day of the visit, not 'later'.
steps.key_takeaway: Three photos. Planogram comparison. Deviation reason. Same-day CRM entry.

quiz.question: What is the 'golden shelf'?
quiz.explanation: Eye level sells the most — it's the spot for bestsellers and premium.

quiz.question: Minimum facings per SKU?
quiz.explanation: Below 2 facings = invisible product. Shoppers don't notice it in the mass.

quiz.question: What does FIFO mean?
options: First In First Out — old to front, new to back
quiz.explanation: Rotation prevents write-offs. FIFO violation = direct loss.

quiz.question: How to group products on the shelf?
quiz.explanation: Shoppers search for 'coffee', not 'Nestle'. Category first, brands within it.

quiz.question: Cross-merchandising is:
quiz.explanation: Cross-merch delivers +8-15% basket — the cheapest sales lift tool.

manager_checklist: Rep knows the planogram and has it in the app
FIFO enforced (check dates on front units)
Proposed cross-merchandising with a relevant category
Took 3 shelf photos and logged CRM entry same-day

kpi.how_to_measure: Facing count vs competitors at audit
